data.title: VRIG: A Bit Disappointing (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This year has proven to be a very tough one for many asset classes, including short-term bond funds. So far, we have the following tally in the markets:

The S&P 500 is down -10% this year, the Nasdaq is down -13%, while the equal weighted Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) is down -7%. The aggregate bond market is slightly up, as illustrated by the iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG).

What was peculiar about this year was the very violent sell-off in the past month driven by tariff announcements. The sell-off saw both equities and long bonds lose value, with a rare positive correlation.

Short-term bond funds were not spared, and in today's article we are going to re-visit the Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF (VRIG), a fund that we last covered two years ago.

Stressed markets drawdown

Let us have a look at how the name did in the recent market stress:

We are comparing VRIG with a cohort formed by the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active Fund (MINT), the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX), the iShares Short Duration Bond ETF (NEAR), the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) and the iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO).

AAA CLOs had by far the largest drawdown, seeing figures at -1.6%. Surprisingly, however, VRIG had the second-largest drawdown, still being down -0.46% in the past month. As expected, pure treasuries were flat, while NEAR is a nice surprise, having a de-minimis drawdown. When compared to overweight bond funds, VRIG disappointed. We are going to have another look at the fund's current holdings and try to identify the factors behind this underperformance.

Current holdings

We are surprised because the fund has a portfolio duration of only 0.22 years, which should keep drawdowns very well contained even in the worst market scenarios.

Since duration is low, and cannot account for nearly a -0.5% drawdown, let us look at the fund components:

  • Corporate bonds: 42%
  • Securitized: 32%
  • Treasuries: 26%

Since corporate bonds have proven to be very liquid, even in this year's drawdown, we are of the opinion that there was slippage in the 'Securitized' bucket when it came down to prices. While the fund is not overweight CLOs in its 'Securitized' bucket, it does contain a large portion of ABS deals, which tend to be more illiquid during market sell-off events.

As a retail investor, it is quite impossible to assess the liquidity of each underlying holding in the fund, thus one has to rely on duration and ratings, but both those elements are looking good for the name:

  • AAA: 39%
  • AA: 15%
  • A: 33%
  • BBB: 11%
  • Other: residual

The fund is overweight in the top portion of the investment grade matrix, thus does not show any glaring issues. We are of the opinion that lower rated deals in the securitized category saw price slippage due to liquidity and wide bid/ask spreads.

Yield considerations

Investors buy short-dated bond funds because they provide for an enhanced yield versus simple treasury or T-Bill funds. VRIG falls in this category:

  • 30-day SEC yield: 4.94%
  • Portfolio yield to worst: 5.6%

The fund therefore offers a 60 bps additional yield when compared to pure T-bill funds. The recent drawdown in the name shows us that the yield enhancement is not for free, with the drawdown roughly equaling the incremental rate. In other words, if an investor panics and sells during a risk-off event, they give up all the additional yield for an entire year.

While the risk/reward ratio is fair, an investor needs to keep that in mind and understand that the additional 60 bps are not necessarily free and come with an increased drawdown risk.

Analytics

  • AUM: $1.2 billion.
  • Sharpe Ratio: 0.8 (3Y).
  • Std. Deviation: 1.2 (3Y).
  • Yield: 4.94%.
  • Premium/Discount to NAV: 0%.
  • Z-Stat: n/a.
  • Leverage Ratio: 0%.
  • Effective Duration: 0.22 years
  • Composition: Short-Term Corporate Bonds

Why the downgrade?

While in a range bound or risk-on market VRIG's drawdown profile is inconsequential, in volatile times it matters. As we have seen recently, the fund can see drawdowns and when compared to a cohort of peers, it has performed a bit subpar. While overall a -0.5% figure is very small, kindly keep in mind many investors bucket this fund as cash, thus might be surprised to see negative figures associated with it. The worst mistake to make is to sell the fund during a risk-off event, a decision which would erase all additional yield for an entire year.

Given the slim margin over T-bills, we no longer find VRIG to be a 'Buy', especially when considering a macro environment fraught with volatility. Until we get some clarity on tariffs and other points from President Trump's agenda, we are going to carefully analyze the risk and rewards associated with any name that engages in yield enhancement strategies.

Conclusion

VRIG is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name offers roughly 60 bps in yield over T-bills, and has performed admirably in calm markets. With the recent market sell-off, we saw the fund experience a -0.5% drawdown, much higher than its peers. While the fund duration is still very low at 0.22 years, the name is now running a bit of liquidity risk via its ABS sleeve, quite similar to what we saw with AAA CLOs. We are therefore downgrading the name to 'Hold' given the view that volatility will not go away this year and the fund will experience further drawdowns.

data.title: Warning: These REITs Are A Dumpster Fire In 2025
data.description:
I am very bullish on REITs (VNQ), and this is no secret to anyone who follows me here on Seeking Alpha. I write weekly bullish articles on various REITs, and this is where I am replying most of my capital at the moment.

However, the REIT sector is vast, and not all REITs are worth buying. Today, we will look at four categories of REITs that I am avoiding because they are either too risky or overpriced. Stick till the end as the one may surprise you.

Hotel REITs

The latest data shows that advanced flight bookings from Canadians coming to the US are down 70% year over year, and tourism from Europe is down 20-50% depending on the country.

Foreigners are showing less interest in coming to the US to spend their money there due to the perception that they are not being respected by the current administration.

On top of that, the threats of major tariffs, coupled with big government cuts, could push the economy into a recession.

This is very bad news for hotels, and it explains why most hotel REITs have been selling off lately.

It is the most cyclical sector of the commercial real estate market because it relies on discretionary spending, and there aren't any long-term leases to protect you.

Making matters worse, this is a sector that requires a lot of capex, and it is facing a growing threat from new technologies like Airbnb (ABNB).

This explains why, historically, the hotel REIT sector has been one of the least rewarding of the entire REIT market:

There will come a point when valuations are low enough to warrant investments into this sector, but we are not quite there yet.

If hotel REITs like Apple Hospitality (APLE) or Host Hospitality (HST) drop by another 10-20%, I will take a closer look.

Mortgage REITs

Mortgage REITs are very popular amongst individual investors because they always offer high dividend yields. To give you an example, AGNC (AGNC) is currently offering a 15% dividend yield!

But if it sounds too good to be true, it probably is, and this is the case here.

Mortgage REITs have performed very poorly over the long run. They would pay big dividends but suffer major capital losses at the same time, resulting in poor total returns:

The main reason for this is that they are commonly overleveraged and too heavily exposed to macro factors that are unpredictable and out of their control.

Small changes to interest rates or spreads can make or break their businesses, but it is near impossible to predict major turns in these factors.

Today, especially, the world we live in is very uncertain, and I wouldn't want the success of my investment to depend on what will happen to interest rates next.

In that sense, investing in mortgage REITs feels more like speculating than investing.

I would add that the surge in interest rates is now starting to put severe stress on even the best mortgage REITs. Even Arbor Realty (ABR) was recently forced to cut its dividend, and I suspect that it may need to cut it again in the near term. A lot of their loans are underwater, and they will likely face significant losses before they eventually recover.

I have no interest in this space.

Office REITs

Now, I am not in the camp of people who claim that offices are going obsolete.

I strongly believe that offices are here to stay, and I am saying this as someone who is running a small business entirely remotely.

I have seen and experienced both, so I know firsthand that there are pros and cons to each.

Offices will increase productivity and collaboration in most people, and it is especially important for a growing company that is training new people.

Therefore, offices are here to stay. There is no doubt about that as more and more big companies like Amazon (AMZN) are now bringing their employees back to the office.

But so why am I not buying office REITs?

They have simply recovered too quickly. As an example, SL Green (SLG) has nearly doubled investors' money over the past two and a half years as it has recovered.

While offices are here to stay, I still think that the sector will face significant pain for many years to come.

Most companies will keep an office but lease less space going forward.

This has put the office market into a severe state of oversupply, with the highest vacancy rates ever recovered at over 20%.

I expect a lot of the lower-quality buildings to change hands in the coming years as the current owners default on them, and as new owners come in with low bases, they will heavily reinvest in them to compete with the latest Class A properties.

Therefore, REITs like SL Green may have avoided most of the pain so far, but I still expect them to be impacted by the oversupply in the years ahead.

Valuations are just too high when taking this into account. I would much rather invest in other property sectors.

Data Center REITs

This one may surprise you.

It seems as if everyone wants to invest in data centers right now to benefit from the growing use of AI, but there's a problem with this.

Data center REITs are pricey.

Increasingly, many of these AI companies want to build their own data centers.

There is a significant risk of oversupply, especially following the latest news of new AI models requiring far less equipment.

Things are just changing too quickly, and I cannot assess whether today's data centers will still be needed to the same degree 20 years from now.

With so much money being pumped into the sector, valuations so high, and technologies evolving so quickly, I just feel that the risk-to-reward of REITs like Iron Mountain (IRM) isn't attractive.

Closing Note

The nice thing about investing in REITs today is that the entire sector is heavily discounted, and therefore, you don't need to invest in these risky sectors to find good value.

Even things like apartment REITs, industrial REITs, and healthcare REITs are heavily discounted.

data.title: PFL: Solid Income Fund But Sensitive To Interest Rates
data.description:
Overview

PIMCO Income Strategy Fund (NYSE:PFL) operates as a closed-end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse debt investments. The fund takes a blended approach between floating and fixed-rate investments and emphasizes income generation. As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, PFL should be able to capitalize by collecting a higher income from its underlying investments. The fund has a public inception dating back to 2003, meaning we have over two decades' worth of performance data to reference.

Looking at the performance over the last year, we can see that PFL's price is essentially at breakeven. The price did recently see a large decline, but this was driven by the market's reaction to President Trump's tariffs. When including all the distributions that were paid out to shareholders, PFL has a total return of over 12% during the same time frame. PFL offers a generous starting dividend yield of 12.1%, making it an attractive choice for income investors. The distributions are paid out on a monthly basis, which can be a nice bonus for retired investors who may depend on the supplemental income generated within their portfolio.

The fund trades at a slight premium to NAV valuation, but this may still be a solid time for entry. The fund has demonstrated that it can generate consistent net investment income and regain some positive NAV momentum when market conditions allow. PFL also has a long history of consistent distributions over time. When the interest rate environment becomes more favorable, PFL may be aligned to reward patient investors who begin accumulating now. So, let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategies utilized by PFL to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PFL has total managed assets of $401M that are spread across approximately 500 different positions. PFL generates its earnings from a diverse mix of debt investments and spreads its focus across different vehicles. High-Yield Credit investments comprise the largest portion of the fund at 30.57%. This is followed by weight in US Government and Non-Agency Mortgage investments, accounting for 14.23% and 11.83% of assets, respectively. The combination of these three areas makes up over 56% of the total fund, so it's safe to assume that these will have the greatest impact on PFL's daily price change and total return potential going forward.

Expanding upon the diversity, PFL focuses these investments across various industries. No specific industry accounts for more than 7% of assets, reinforcing just how diverse the fund is. Wireless investments constitute the largest bulk of the fund at 6.88%. This is followed by a 6% weight in Healthcare and a 5.87% weight in Technology.

Since PFL focuses on debt investments, we can assume that a significant portion of the fund's investments operate on a floating rate basis. As interest rates increase, the debt maintenance or interest expense that borrowers need to pay will increase. PFL benefits from this since the fund can collect a higher amount of interest income from its borrowers. The only issue is that it becomes more difficult for underlying investments to increase in value when the cost of debt is inflated, which limits PFL's share price growth.

Lastly, PFL utilizes leverage as part of its underlying strategy to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. The leverage currently amounts to 10.91% of assets, which I consider to be quite conservative. Since we remain in a higher interest rate environment, it would be better to keep leverage usage low while the cost of debt may be elevated. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if used on a poor investment.

Valuation

Since PFL operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying net assets. As a result, PFL currently trades at a slight premium to the NAV of 3.08%. For reference, PFL has traded at an average premium to NAV of 4.12% over the last three-year period. I've noticed that income-focused funds usually trade at a consistent premium due to the consistency in earnings or NAV stability that they can provide. The premium or discount is typically a direct reflection of what the market thinks about the fund. Looking at a PFL over the last decade, the fund has consistently traded in the premium territory.

In an effort to get more clarity on the fundamentals of PFL, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report dated as of December 2024. Net investment income landed at $0.42 per share, while net realized gains amounted to $0.35 per share for the first half of the fiscal year. This resulted in a net growth from operations of $0.77 per share, which puts PFL in a great position so far. Looking over the prior years of performance, PFL has been able to consistently generate positive net investment income year after year.

However, net realized gains seem to be at the mercy of whatever direction market indices are going. I would like to see PFL reach a point where the net investment income can fully support the distribution amounts on its own because it would reduce the fund's reliance on net realized gains. Despite the reliance, PFL has been able to steadily regain its NAV momentum after reaching a low in 2023. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $11.00
  • 2020: $9.46
  • 2021: $10.66
  • 2022: $8.39
  • 2023: $7.77
  • 2024: $7.84
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $8.13

While the fund still hasn't reached its pre-pandemic NAV level, I believe that it's a possibility when conditions become more favorable. The fact that PFL has demonstrated that it's capable of regaining its NAV could justify the small premium at the moment. If we see a scenario where interest rates fall and stimulate more borrowing activity, PFL would be aligned for growth. The only issue is that the outlook for interest rate cuts remains uncertain.

Risk Profile

Although higher interest rates can be great for income generation, it can also increase the risk profile of an income fund like PFL. A higher interest rate means that borrowers may feel the strain from increased interest expenses in the form of lower operating margins. This can make it more difficult to grow and cause businesses to shift their focus on capital efficiency, rather than pursuing growth initiatives that can drive valuations higher.

This may be why we see that PFL has an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate below. When rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the pandemic, the price of PFL rapidly moved upward since debt was so cheap, and it was a lot easier for borrowers to grow in valuation. Borrowers could use cheap debt financing as a way to fuel things like acquisitions, product development, and research, or fund marketing campaigns that increase sales.

Conversely, we can see how PFL's price rapidly retracted to the downside throughout 2022 and 2023 as the Fed aggressively raised interest rates to combat record-high inflationary data. Therefore, we can estimate that future interest rate cuts would serve as a positive catalyst for growth in the future. The only issue is that there is a lot of uncertainty related to the future of interest rate cuts. The Fed is still trying to determine how tariffs will affect the market and the American people.

Additionally, inflation rates remain higher than anticipated, so the Fed only estimates there to be one interest rate cut. However, I believe that there's also a possibility that interest rates could increase over the next twelve months, which could further suppress PFL's price and delay the growth back to pre-pandemic levels.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0814 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.1%. Looking at the dividend history over the last decade, PFL has done a decent job at maintaining consistent payouts. There was a slight decrease over the course of 2021 due to lower earnings, however. Despite the decrease in the past, I don't think management has a reason to cut the dividends in the near term. If we take the monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.9744 per share. PFL hasn't had an issue generating earnings exceeding this amount in the past. PFL has already earned $0.77 per share as of the last semi-annual report.

However, investors should be aware that the distributions received from PFL are typically classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the heaviest tax consequences, compared to the qualified dividend income you may receive from more traditional dividend-paying stocks. Based on your specific tax bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in PFL may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any tax burdens.

There have also been instances where the return of capital was used. During periods where PFL may not actually generate enough income to support distributions, it may use the return of capital to make up the difference. These distributions aren't directly taxed, but an investor's cost basis is reduced instead. This essentially defers taxes until the time of sale. The downside is that these distributions have a direct negative effect on the NAV and can accelerate NAV deterioration.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate PFL as a hold for now due to the uncertainty around the interest rate environment. Interest rate cuts could serve as a positive catalyst for growth, but it doesn't seem like the Fed will be initiating sizable cuts anytime soon. There is still a lot of uncertainty in the markets related to the changing tariffs and inflationary data. Despite these challenges, the fund has proven that it can remain resilient. The NAV fell over recent years due to the price decline caused by higher interest rates, but there has been some positive momentum over the last year. Net investment income levels have remained fairly consistent over the last few years. If the fund can eventually increase net investment income levels, it may take away from the reliance on net realized gains to fuel growth.

data.title: Spotify: A Great Company, But With Too Much Growth Priced In
data.description:
Today, I want to take a look at Spotify Technology (SPOT) and understand why I rate the company as a STRONG SELL. Don't get me wrong here; I do love Spotify, use their product daily, and believe that they have a great business model. The thing is that currently they are extremely overvalued compared to their future growth potential. Therefore, my thesis is that either the company's stock price will fall in the near future, or if it doesn't, it won't bring too much return to investors as future growth is already priced in. To properly understand my thesis and rating, we will first go through the company's recent advances, to then look at its fundamentals and finally calculate their intrinsic value through 2 of our intrinsic valuation models.

Introduction

Before jumping to the analysis, let's introduce the company. First of all, I want to establish how the company generates revenue. They have two main income sources: Ad Revenue and Premium subscriptions, with 12.00% of their revenue being generated by ads and 88.00% generated through MRR (Monthly Reoccurring Revenue). Now what you have to understand is that even though MRR accounts for the majority of the company's revenue generating abilities, it comes from only 38.96% of all MAUs (Monthly Active Users); the rest, or the 12.00%, comes from the 61.04% free users. Through this, we can easily deduce that the company is benefiting much more from premium users than from ad revenue.

Analysis

Market Overview

Understanding this, let's get to the analysis. The company operates both in the US and internationally, with 27.00% of their MAUs coming from Europe, 17.00% from NA, 22.00% from SA, and 34.00% from the rest of the world. Overall, in 2024, the company recorded 675.00M MAUs, which is a 12.00% YOY Increase.

The first thing I want to point out is that with around 8.00B people currently walking the surface of the earth, with their 675.00M MAUs, Spotify currently accommodates only 8.44% of all people. This might sound like a good thing, as they still have a lot of potential customers to reach, but, what I want to point out is that more than 80.00% of their premium revenue and 60.00% of ad revenue come from first-world countries, which account for only around 15.00% of the global population.

This would mean that currently, Spotify has already reached more than half of its highest revenue generating customers. Furthermore, above you can see that through the last 5 years, the highest growth in MAUs has been recorded from "rest of the world," which, for the most part, are not first world countries, and so it can be observed that the premium subscription growth for this geographic has stayed quite low. In other words, in 2024, although the "rest of the world" users accounted for more than a third of the company's MAUs they accounted for only 14.00% of their MRR from premium subscriptions.

Regarding the company's overall growth, they have been doing an excellent job. From Q1 '22, their MAUs grew by 58.82%, accounting for an average QOQ growth of 4.06%. Though what is in my eyes slightly disappointing is that through the same time period, so from Q1 '22 to Q4 '24, the number of premium subscribers has increased by only around 23.72%, while non-premium users have increased by around 35.10%. This supports the point I made earlier, being that the geographical segment with the most users, growing at the highest rate, also generates the least revenue.

Nevertheless, this certainly doesn't mean that the company is doing bad; on the contrary, they are growing really well. The issue is that they are currently overvalued compared to their future growth potential. To understand this further, we will jump to the 9 Pillar Analysis, but before that, let's take a quick look at the company's guidance.

Guidance

In Q1 of 2025, the total MAUs are expected to increase from 675.00M to 678.00M, so a 0.44% increase. Premium subscribers are expected to increase by 0.73% from 263.00M to 265.00M, with revenue expected to climb by 90bps to €4.20B.

So overall, it is obvious that the company is growing, and with their momentum not showing a sign of stopping, with their increasing popularity I would expect them to grow into the future, but now let's take a look at their fundamental health.

9 Pillar Analysis

Before we go through the 9 Pillar Analysis, let me explain what it is and how it works. Pillars 1 and 2 are the 5Y PE and P/FCF ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4 and 5 depict the 5Y revenue, net income and FCF growth, with Pillar 6 showing the 5-year profit margin and Pillar 7 the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 shows the dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio, which helps determine a company's ability to pay down all their debt within a safe period using their FCF while paying out dividends.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

Firstly, let's take a look at the company's revenue growth. From 2020, it has grown by 98.90%, increasing from €7.88B to €15.67B in 2024. This accounts for a high CAGR of 15.05%.

Regarding the net income, that has increased from - €581.00M in 2020, to €1.14B in 2024, accounting for an adjusted CAGR of 37.90%. What we have to be wary about through, is that 2024 was the company's first and so far, only year in the positive regarding net income; therefore, we can't really deduce a stable future CAGR.

Nevertheless, their FCF has been growing really nicely, moving up from €181.00M in 2020, to €2.28B in 2024.

This means that currently, their FCF/S is sitting nice and high at 11.03. But that doesn't mean that they are fairly valued. To figure that out, let's take a look at their P/FCF and PE ratios.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Regarding the company's PE, as for most of the past 5Y, their net income has been negative; their 5Y PE is sitting at -54.46. The current PE is also sitting at a rather overvalued level, at 91.73, which would mean that investors are paying $91.73 for one dollar of the company's earnings, which for me, even though the company has great potential, is extremely overvalued.

But to really understand the valuation, I like to look at the P/FCF, as unlike the net income, which is essentially just a measuring metric, the FCF is the actual amount of money a company has, and can use for share buybacks, paying down debt, paying out a dividend, etc.

Looking at the adjusted 5Y P/FCF, it is sitting at 80.40, which is much higher than the current ratio of 51.89, showing that the company is becoming cheaper. Though when you look at the graph below, you can see that through 2023 to 2024, the PE was sitting quite lower than it is currently, meaning that actually, the company has become much more expensive through the past 2 years.

Overall, even though the company is expected to grow at a decent rate into the future, I believe that at this price, there has been too much growth priced in, growth that the company won't be able to accomplish. Nevertheless, before calculating the intrinsic value, we still have a few more pillars to go through, so now let's jump to pillar 7, the shares outstanding.

Shares Outstanding (Pillar 7)

Something I do want to point out, as I find it really important, is that through the past 5Y, Spotify has increased their shares outstanding by 10.11%, leading to a CAGR of 1.97%, increasing them from 181.00M in 2020 to 204.00M currently. Now, 6.15% of the shares were sold out in 2024, which, when looking into the cash flow statement, would mean that around $1.00B ($966.50M) of the company's 2024 FCF was generated through share sales.

To understand this better, selling out shares is a short-term way to raise capital, but through the long term, it takes value away from investors. Therefore, I do not like the fact that most of the company's 2024 FCF came from share sellouts, as this FCF generating method is not sustainable. On the other hand, it is quite understandable that Spotify sold out this many shares, as currently, YOY their P/FCF is up by around 100.00%, which in my eyes shows that they took advantage of trading above their historical value to sell expensive and later potentially buy back cheap.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Finally, let's take a look at the debt levels of the company. Their LTL/5Y FCF ratio is sitting at 1.76, which meant that with their average 5Y FCF, they wouldn't have an issue paying off the €2.03B in LTL they currently hold. Furthermore, I do expect the FCF to grow into the future, and therefore this ratio will be lower, meaning that Spotify is quite well protected from financial downturns.

Overall, the 9 Pillars are looking quite decent. The revenue is growing at a high CAGR, and the company has manageable debt. The thing I don't like though is that, as we just saw, most of the company's 2024 FCF was generated through share sellouts, meaning that it hasn't increased through the growth of the company's product. Furthermore, they seem quite overvalued both compared to their PE and P/FCF, and so now, let's figure out their intrinsic value with certainty by predicting the company's future EPS and FCF.

Intrinsic Value

First, we will move to the Graham's Formula. Here I will plug in the current EPS of $6.24 and predict a really high EPS CAGR of 35.00%. This will give me an intrinsic value of $327.38, 42.80% lower than the current trading price.

To understand this overvaluation better, let's reverse the analysis. For the company to be worth $572.39 per share, currently an EPS CAGR of 64.67% is priced in, which in my eyes is unachievable.

For the second valuation model, I want to look at the DCF model. Here we will base the intrinsic value on the future FCF growth. For starters, I will plug in a base FCF of $1.29B, as the 5Y average is in my eyes too low, and the 2024 FCF is too heavily affected by the share sellouts ($1.29B is the average between the 5Y FCF and the 2024 FCF). Based on this FCF, I will calculate the FFCF using a CAGR of 21.00% into the next 10Y +. I will then discount all of this FFCF at a rate of 8.67% and get an intrinsic value of $369.29.

Finally, we will take the two values and average them out to $348.33, which is 39.14% lower than the current trading price, supporting my STRONG SELL rating.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I do have to rate Spotify Technology as a STRONG SELL. This decision is based on the high overvaluation compared both to the current and future net income and FCF. Although the company has been showcasing great growth both in their revenue and MAUs, and I expect them to grow into the future, I still find it insufficient for the high growth priced in. As I have stated prior, I do find Spotify a great company with a great model; I just don't agree with their current valuation and believe that in the future their stock price will either fall or showcase sow growth. Therefore, my rating stands as a STRONG SELL.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Best Inflation Hedge In Today's Turbulent Market
data.description:
The U.S. stock market has been experiencing tremendous volatility since President Trump announced the tariffs for many countries. Though I thought this would be over when he paused these tariffs for 90 days and gave an electronics examination for tariffs on China, analysts say this volatility will continue until the market gets a clear direction of policies. Risks of recession and inflation are on the horizon. Goldman Sachs says there is a 45% risk of recession in the U.S. economy.

Amid this situation, investors are searching for safe havens in stocks that are defensive against inflation.

I believe the consumer staples sector is the one we call a winner in the recent volatility of the broader index. S&P 500 dipped nearly 8% in 2025, while the Consumer Staples sector has rallied about 4% in this time frame, which only tells the story of resilience.

Cut to the chase. I believe Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) is the one stock sitting at the top with a defensive business model that is a perfect shield from inflation. The company has strong fundamentals, pricing power, and steady cash flows, which offer a safe haven when other stocks get hit by market volatility.

For instance, the stock performed extremely well in volatile conditions. It is up 17% year-to-date and 24% over the past 52 weeks. In comparison, its biggest rivals, PepsiCo (PEP) and Monster Beverage (MNST), are lagging behind with a 12% loss and an 8% surge in the same time period.

Although the stock trades at premium levels, I remain bullish on the stock because I think paying a premium for a highly profitable dividend stock is worth it.

Coca-Cola Tariff Immune?

As we all know, tariffs are temporary pauses that are not fully lifted, so the effects of these tariffs always remain intact. To be honest, I think there are some elements of a company that can be affected by looming tariffs. For example, in the operations area, I feel there could be some problems. Imported raw materials and packaging components could raise production costs, and hence, this could affect profitability. Specifically, increased duties on aluminum and certain agricultural inputs used in beverages might strain margins in the short term.

However, I believe that the company's global presence and diversified supply chain will help the company navigate these challenges. As the beverage giant sources materials from multiple regions, it will help to dilute the impact of these tariffs, which are concentrated on specific markets.

Additionally, as we have seen, the company's long-standing supplier agreements and smart financing have helped the company a lot in the past to handle cost increases whenever they were hit by trade policy changes. So, I believe this will also help them in the future to handle tariff risks.

In short, I think while the company could see temporary profitability issues in the near term, its smart supply chain and strong market position mean it's likely to handle the 2025 tariffs without major problems.

Financial Overview

The above-mentioned graph indicates that in the past 5 years, the company has grown consistently both in terms of revenue and gross profit. This is the reason behind the consecutive growth of the company. KO aims to make things happen using strategic pricing, product innovation, and global presence, and Coca-Cola gives the customer what he or she needs. Persisting high growth in the company's Zero Sugar category, 9% annually in 2024 and 13% in the fourth quarter, versus 6% in total net revenue, up 6% in that same quarter.

Indeed, the company has topped both revenue and estimates in its latest quarterly results and also achieved 12% EPS growth, landing at $0.51 per share. Total revenue arrived at $11.5 billion, beating the estimate of $10.68 billion.

The company also saw a 14% rise in its organic revenue, mainly due to its higher prices. Prices went up 9%, with 4% of that increase coming from countries facing very high inflation. The rest came from regular price hikes and customers choosing more expensive products.

The best thing about the company is its profit margins. The company has a 61% gross margin, almost 70% above the sector median of 36%. This shows that Coca-Cola operates with exceptional efficiency and pricing power, allowing it to generate significantly higher profitability compared to its peers in the beverage sector.

Even more impressive is the EBIT margin, which has an A+ grade and currently stands at 30.39%. This figure is 220% above the sector median of 9.45%. This shows how well the company is managing its operational and labor costs while improving scalability.

Moreover, KO is the cash flow-generating machine. The company has an outstanding levered free cash flow ((FCF)) margin of 31.79%, which suggests KO is generating exceptional cash flow even after covering financial obligations.

As seen in its balance sheet, Coca-Cola maintains a steady debt pattern that has not grown substantially during recent years, since it manages sufficient cash generation for establishing new geographical markets.

Talking about cash flows, the company ended 2024 with a healthy $15.9 billion free cash flow, which is staggering, almost 180% up from last year. It also turned its net cash in hand positive to $1.79 billion from last year's negative of $133 million.

This all happens because the company is adding over 250,000 net new outlets and nearly 600,000 new coolers. This unbelievable expansion boosted the company's availability worldwide. Also, its disciplined capital allocation helped the company give $8.4 billion in dividends, which shows the company's commitment to awarding shareholders.

As we look ahead, analysts are calling for continued EPS growth for the company, except in Q1 2025, because of the tariff fears and currency fluctuations.

However, in the other remaining three quarters, we can see that the growth of 3.5% and 7.9% is stated by analysts, which is quite a good number in this sector, where growth is comparable to other sectors like tech.

Dividend King

Coca-Cola is an excellent dividend-paying company. Warren Buffett made millions of dollars from this stock only by receiving dividends. As you can see, the upper graph only shows the growth of the past 11 years, but you will not believe that Coca-Cola has consistently raised dividends in the past 62 years.

Indeed, the company holds the title of Dividend King, having raised its payouts each year for the last 50 years. I think this is a truly safe investment for retired people who are looking for stable passive income with consistent growth. The company is now paying a $2.04 annual dividend, which pays out in quarterly phases of $0.51. The company is currently yielding at a healthy 2.94%, which is 16% higher than the sector median of 2.54%.

Valuation Analysis

The valuation appears alarming according to the red marks shown on the graph. The current metrics for KO correspond with the standard 5-year historical averages. The high market valuation exists because Coca-Cola has maintained operating excellence and delivered consistent financial results over a decade.

For my Coca‑Cola cash flow prediction, I used a discounted cash flow model, yet my main focus centered on determining both the company's consistent free cash flow generation rate and its foreseeable sustainable growth rate. The mature business profile of Coca‑Cola led me to create projected free cash flow calculations spanning five years through the usage of careful single-digit annual forecasts. The analysis used an 11% discount rate together with terminal growth rates between 2% and 3%, which fit within national market expectations.

An evaluation of Coca‑Cola indicates the company should reach a $36 per share valuation, while investors place the stock at $72 in today's market. Coca‑Cola demonstrates evidence of being significantly overpriced according to the findings of my current DCF valuation, while relying on defined projection assumptions.

Investors will argue that if the stock is overvalued, why buy now? The answer is that the stock has been overvalued for the last 5 years, and there is no guarantee that this valuation will come down in the future because, as we have seen, the stock showed resilience even in the worst market situations. So, if investors invest in this best-performing beverage company, they have to pay a premium.

Risks to my case

There are a few risks in this investment, and we will break them down one by one.

The first is overvaluation. If the company does not deliver decent returns in the future, there is a risk that investors may face significant downside corrections.

Secondly, tariffs may cause the prices of raw materials and other components to rise, which can affect the company's profitability margins in the near term. While Coca-Cola employs long-term contracts and diversified supplier relationships to mitigate this risk, disruptions or sustained price increases remain a concern.

Despite the strong fundamentals, there could be a negative trend driven by market volatility. However, consumer staple stocks don't get hit as hard as sector stocks like tech, but the negative impact of a broader market collapse could still hurt the stock's performance.

Lastly, analysts' prediction of an EPS decline in the next quarter will likely intensify the pressure from rivals like PepsiCo and Monster Beverage. These earnings decline may cause a hurdle to the company's business stability.

Takeaway

I believe Coca-Cola is the one stock that checks all the right boxes with solid fundamentals, consistent stock growth, and a decent dividend policy. Although the valuation is expensive, I believe it's worth investing in, as the company offers so many perks with its premium pricing.

Plus, as we have seen, the stock does not get shaky in extremely volatile conditions, which means it will provide stable growth in the upcoming years. Its defensive business model, robust pricing power, and consistent cash flow make it a reliable investment choice in uncertain economic conditions.

Moreover, thankfully, the stock is trading well above its 50-day and 200-day moving averages, which shows a bullish trend. I believe this will continue in 2025 with the help of strong operations and fundamentals.

Last but not least, KO holds a "Buy" consensus in Wall Street analyst ratings, with a 4.3 rating.

data.title: uniQure: AMT-130 For HD Progresses Forward With Q2 2025 FDA Meeting
data.description:
The last time I wrote an article on uniQure (NASDAQ:QURE) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "uniQure: Positive HD AMT-130 Treatment Data Leads To Several Other Catalysts". With respect to this article, I already laid out that it had achieved positive 24-month interim data showing that its gene therapy was able to establish an 80% slowing of disease progression in patients with Huntington's Disease (HD). I also mentioned that the company was set to meet with the FDA in a meeting to discuss a possible Accelerated Approval pathway of AMT-130 for the treatment of these patients. I'm happy to report that an initial positive update with the FDA, through a Type B meeting, ended up happening in Q1 of 2025.

However, this was only the basis for discussing the Chemistry, Controls, and Manufacturing portion of the Biologics License Application (BLA) submission. Thus, there is a catalyst opportunity here with respect to this program. The company is set to meet with the FDA in Q2 of 2025 to discuss a pivotal statistical analysis plan to move this program forward. The initial meeting went well in that it was noted that the phase 1/2 study comparing the drug to an external control may be used for the Accelerated Approval pathway. Along with two other items, will go over below.

A more recent news item based on this program is that AMT-130 for the treatment of patients was given FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation (BTD). Prior to this, this gene therapy was also given the Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (RMAT) designation. The company is also already moving forward with another program from its pipeline, which is the development of gene therapy AMT-191. This particular candidate is being developed to treat patients with Fabry Disease. A good update was also provided here a few months ago, whereby the independent data monitoring committee (IDMC) already recommended dosing of the second cohort of this study.

AMT-130 Set For Additional FDA Meeting Catalyst Q2 Of 2025

As I stated in the prior article, the company did really well in terms of its phase 1/2 study using its gene therapy AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with Huntington's Disease (HD). That's because, with the release of the interim analysis of 24 months, it was noted that patients who took the high dose of this therapy achieved an 80% slowing of disease progression. This was shown compared to that of those instead from a propensity-score weighted external control group. The slowing of disease progression was measured by the composite Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (cUHDRS) at this 24-month period. Another finding was that patients who took AMT-130 had CSF neurofilament light protein (NfL) lowered in a statistically significant manner. In essence, patients were able to have this biomarker lowered to be below baseline over this 24-month treatment period. Why do I bring up both of these efficacy measures from this study? Besides having been shown to be positive from this phase 1/2 study, it is because both of these endpoints are what uniQure discussed with the FDA about potential Accelerated Approval. That is, in the Type B RMAT meeting held with the FDA in December 2024, it was noted that both NfL and cUHDRS could be used as measures to obtain such Accelerated Approval of AMT-130 for the treatment of these patients with Huntington's Disease. Along with the fact that the ongoing phase 1/2 study comparing this therapy to a natural history could serve as the basis for a BLA filing.

Having said all of this, there is a catalyst opportunity for investors to focus on, which is the next FDA meeting set to take place. That's right, the company is expected to meet with the FDA again in Q2 of 2025. Afterward, it is going to provide further guidance on a potential Accelerated Approval pathway of AMT-130 for the treatment of these patients with HD. It will also be in a position to provide guidance on when it might actually expect to file a BLA for this gene therapy for the treatment of these patients as well. The hope is that uniQure is able to get this drug to market as quickly as possible. Why is that? That's because, for the time being, there are no disease-modifying therapies approved for these HD patients. The catalyst I just laid out is actually only one of many. As a matter of fact, there are three other milestones for investors to look forward to. The first of which, expected in the 1st half of 2025, is an interaction with the European Medicines Agency (EMA) so that it could discuss a potential filing of a regulatory application of AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with HD for the marketing of this therapy in the European territories. Along with the fact that there is going to be an updated release of safety data from Cohort 3 of the ongoing phase 1/2 study during the first half of this year. Another huge catalyst is going to be that of long-term data from Cohorts 1 and 2 from this very same trial. Consider that this is going to be crucial because it will be known if a one-time gene therapy like AMT-130 can achieve a long-lasting, durable response. In essence, this is going to be 3-year follow-up data to be released in mid-2025.

Huntington's Disease is characterized as a rare, inherited neurodegenerative disorder whereby nerve cells in a person's brain break down and die off. This is a highly progressive disease, whereby multiple aspects of a person's body are affected. It can have an impact on thoughts, thinking, and movement. Here is the kicker, there are actually two types of HD, and they are as follows:

  • Adult Onset Huntington's Disease—This is far more common of the two types, and symptoms from it typically show up after age 30
  • Juvenile Huntington's Disease—Affects teenagers and children, but is highly rare.

The reason for this disease coming about is because of a gene that is located on chromosome 4 that is mutated or abnormal. In good standing, it is not clear what the gene does when it is considered to be “normal”. However, when it is mutated, it has been identified to cause HD. Such a gene has a CAG repeat sequence, an excessive amount that drives disease. Where does AMT-130 come into play here? Well, AMT-130 is made up of an AAV5 vector that carries an artificial microRNA (mRNA) to silence the Huntington gene. Thus, the ultimate goal is to inhibit the production of mutant (mHTT) protein. The global Huntington's Disease Treatment market is expected to reach $2.03 billion by 2033. This is a good market opportunity and uniQure with AMT-130 has the potential to have the best and first-in-class type of therapy for them.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, uniQure had cash, cash equivalents, and investments of $367.5 million as of December 31st, 2024. However, in Q1 of 2025, it completed an offering; raising a total of $80.7 million in net proceeds. It sold a total of 4,411,764 of its ordinary shares at a public offering price of $17.00 per share. This means the pro-forma cash position with the newly completed offering was approximately $448 million. The company is expected to be able to fund its operations for an extended period of time. Why do I make such a claim? It is because it notes in its earnings release that it believes it has enough to fund its operations through the 2nd half of 2027. The company burns approximately $49.12 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $35.95 in R&D expenses and then $13.17 in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in uniQure. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the regulatory update that it is expected to provide in Q2 of 2025 for the advancement of AMT-130 for the treatment of patients with HD. The reason why is that it is set to meet with the FDA in a Type B meeting in Q2. Such a meeting is going to be to see if the company will definitely be in a position to file a BLA for this gene therapy to treat these patients. The risk here is that the FDA could change its stance on the company's ability to file a BLA for this program. If this happens, not only would it take longer to get AMT-130 marketed for HD, but in the short term it would cause the stock price to trade lower.

A second risk to consider would be in terms of another program that the company is working on for its pipeline. This would be what I noted above, which is the development of AMT-191 for Fabry Disease. The IDMC allowed the company to move to dosing of Cohort 2 for this particular trial, and this was based on no observation of issues in Cohort 1. With that said, the dosing of Cohort 2 is expected to happen in Q2 of 2025. After which, it believes it will be in a position to release updated data from this phase 1/2 study in the 2nd half of 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data to be released then will be positive, for starters. In addition, while no safety issues were observed in Cohort 1 of this trial, it doesn't mean that there won't be any adverse events or dose-limiting toxicities (DLTs) observed in the next cohort (Cohort 2).

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of gene therapy AMT-162 for the treatment of patients with SOD1 Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). The use of this AAV5 gene therapy is being evaluated to treat this patient population in the ongoing phase 1/2 EPISODE1 study. The company is expected to provide a data update from this specific study in the first half of 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that AMT-162 will be able to adequately address SOD1 ALS patients in an effective manner for starters. Secondly, there is no assurance that the data to be generated from this early-stage study is going to be enough to warrant further investigation of this AAV5 gene therapy in another clinical study.

Conclusion

uniQure has done well to continue with the development of its AMT-130 AAV5 gene therapy to treat patients with Huntington's Disease. That's because it is set in line for another FDA Type B meeting in Q2 of 2025. Should the outcome of this meeting go in a positive way, then it should be able to file a BLA for this gene therapy based on an Accelerated Approval pathway. It is important to note that the timing of when it will potentially submit a BLA will not be known until the company provides an update in Q2 of 2025 after such a meeting. With no disease-modifying therapies in place to help these patients, the hope is that the FDA will stick to what was noted beforehand, which is that the cUHDRS and NfL efficacy endpoints can be used for Accelerated Approval of AMT-130 for the treatment of these HD patients. The company is not only relying on the advancement of this program from its pipeline, but it is also developing other AAV5 gene therapies as well. These would be what I went over above, which are AMT-191 for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease and AMT-162 for the targeting of SOD1 ALS patients. With another Type B meeting update with the FDA on possible Accelerated Approval of AMT-130 for HD in Q2 of 2025, plus the release of 3-year follow-up data from cohorts 1 & 2 from the phase 1/2 study in HD patients in mid-2025, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: JD.com: Enduring Growth Amid A Trade War, Hiking My Target Price
data.description:
Chinese stocks have outperformed notably so far in 2025. The iShares China Large-Cap ETF (FXI) is up by more than 7%, dividends included, year to date, while the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has returned –9.4%. Shares of JD.com (JD) have moved in lockstep with its country ETF. Ahead of earnings next month, I reiterate a buy rating.

I was bullish on JD toward the end of 2024, shortly after the stock soared on the September-October stimulus news. Unfortunately, shares are down 15%, total return, since early November. I did note though, that buying the dip on a pullback to $33 would work well given chart trends, and I’ll underscore why that remains a key technical spot.

Fundamentally, I have adjustments to the valuation that I will detail.

China (FXI) Posts Alpha Versus the S&P 500 ETF YTD

Back in March, JD reported a very solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPADS of $1.02 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.83, while revenue of $47.5 billion, up more than 13% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $1.83 billion beat.

Margins were the big story, and continued healthy operating leverage should promote strong earnings as 2025 presses on. Shares were hardly changed after the Q4 report, and the options market prices in a 6.9% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the early-May reporting date.

Looking closer at the previous quarter, the $51 billion market cap Broadline Retail industry company within the Consumer Discretionary sector ended 2024 with a flourish. The 4%-plus margin came with strong sales growth in electronics and appliances, though that was boosted, in part, by government subsidies. JD is often framed as a volatile consumer stock, but double-digit growth in supermarket and services offers a less volatile area of its total sales mix.

On the call, the management team remained optimistic that further government stimulus and subsidies would be on the way. All the while, the Beijing-based firm is investing in AI and robotics to bring down costs and increase efficiencies, which should help the margin story in 2025. For a better top line, its newly launched food delivery service should scale with JD’s existing infrastructure and operations.

JD is also a shareholder’s friend. During the last quarterly update, the company announced a $1.5 billion dividend and a new $5 billion buyback program, all while forecasting high single-digit revenue growth this year. Tariffs are a key risk, but JD is less exposed than some of its retailer peers.

Overall, it was a fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth in quarterly active customers, and the management team reaffirmed its 2025 outlook. As for the earnings picture outside of the company offices, analysts expect steady EPS gains in 2025 through the out year and FY 2025. Revenue is seen rising between 5% and 8%. The sell-side is indeed upbeat—there have been 26 earnings upgrades in the past 90 days compared with a lone downgrade.

What I particularly like is that JD’s free cash flow per share is high at $2.58 over the past 12 months, resulting in a FCF yield of 7.3%--much better than most US retailers. Broader profitability trends are stellar, too.

JD: Revenue & Earnings Estimates, EPS Revision Trends

With a healthy fundamental growth backdrop, I have updates to my valuation. I am hiking my forward-12-month EPS assumption from $4.05 to $4.80 based on the refreshed forecasts.

I’ll stick with a 12x multiple for now, but that could increase if we see further attraction from investors toward China. The intrinsic value target is now $58, making JD significantly discounted. Shares also trade at half their historical price-to-sales ratio.

JD: Depressed Valuation Multiples, Even Compared to the Stock's Own History

Being a Chinese stock, JD has obvious risks—it’s among the Terrific 10. Unknowns about what the Chinese government will do with its tech and consumer companies are a potentially perilous situation—we saw that from 2021 through early last year. Moreover, a global macroeconomic slowdown would surely hurt the currently sanguine growth trajectory.

Also, JD operates within a highly competitive industry, so new entrants could swipe share or simply lead to weaker margins. A trade war would hurt critical supply chains, too, while the food delivery penetration is not a sure winner—we'll find out more in the Q2 report in May.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon shows an unconfirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Thursday, May 15 BMO. A $1.00 per share dividend is paid out on April 29. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With the stock even more undervalued in my estimation compared to late in 2024, JD’s technical chart is mixed. Notice in the graph below that shares remain well below where they were in October last year and this past March. The rising long-term 200-day moving average is now fractionally above the current stock price, and long-term support at $32 is in play. So, there’s a key confluence of technical support points to watch right now.

I called out $32 last November, suggesting that it would be an ideal place to ‘buy the dip.’ That has worked out well so far, but if JD loses that key level, then the next downside target would be the breakout point at the end of the third quarter of 2024—near $26.

I’d like to see the stock hold the 200dma and rally on improved RSI momentum. Being an ADR, JD is wont to gap up and down, so I would downplay the significance of gaps. More important resistance is seen between $46 and $48.

JD: Shares Retreat to Critical $32 Support, $48 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on JD. With a single-digit P/E, big free cash flow, healthy margins, shareholder-friendly actions, and technical support in play, the future looks bright with the Q2 earnings report on the horizon.

data.title: Sell MSOS And Its 3 Largest Holdings
data.description:
I was optimistic for the multi-state operators when cannabis rescheduling by the DEA was underway, and I upgraded to Neutral some of them and the world's largest cannabis ETF, the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:MSOS). Things have not appeared to be moving towards the elimination of 280E taxation, which will be devastating to the American cannabis industry if it remains. Today, I am downgrading three large MSOs, and I am also downgrading MSOS again.

When I wrote about MSOS four weeks ago, I did downgrade it to sell and say that it was at big risk. While it is down since then, I am changing my rating to Strong Sell. I am also downgrading its three largest holdings, Curaleaf (OTCPK:CURLF), Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF) and Trulieve (OTCQX:TCNNF).

MSOS Is Still In a Bear Market

MSOS has dropped a ton, but it is not at the low. In fact, this move last week helped it to fill an open gap that had been left in early April:

MSOS and its MSO holdings all plunged in November when Florida failed to pass adult-use legalization, which needed 60% of voters but fell short, and Trump was elected President. I had thought the market was overdone then, but it has continued to sell off.

Over the past 4+ years, MSOS has plunged almost 90%, and it has pulled back even more from its peak:

Trading volumes have plunged too. Few seem to care about this ETF compared to how many did in the past.

MSOS Has Seen Redemptions

At the beginning of the year, MSOS had 130.575 million shares outstanding. Today, there are 132.125 million, so the share count has increased by 1.2%. This is after it rose 52.5% in 2024. Shares have decreased since March 7th by 3.4%. This is not that bad considering how much the price has declined since then (11.7%) and year-to-date (32.8%). Since August 2023, shares have increased, but the one-year growth of 14.1% is the lowest it has been and down from the peak of 94% during the summer of 2024:

So, the one-year growth in shares outstanding has plunged, and the number of shares outstanding has dropped since early March.

Sell MSOS and Its Top Holdings

MSOS does not trade at a big premium to its net asset value, but it has rallied sharply from its all-time low earlier this month. The current price of $2.56 is a penny above its NAV and is up 26.7% from its all-time intraday low of $2.02. The bounces at its three top holdings have been 45.2% for Curaleaf, 21.0% for GTI and 28.2% for Trulieve. Of course, MSOS is down a lot in 2025, falling 32.8%. CURLF has dropped 37.2%, GTBIF has fallen 31.5%, and TCNNF is down 25.3%. The overall cannabis sector has fallen year-to-date by 28.1% as measured by the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index.

Year-to-date, MSOS has seen its shares outstanding increase by 1.2%, though they fell last week again and in March too. The redemptions this past week hit each of the three top holdings. Year-to-date, MSOS exposure to GTBIF has dropped by 11.8%. It has increased its exposure to CURLF shares by 7.0%, and it has decreased exposure to TCNNF shares by 1.3%. Here is the current portfolio's top-10 holdings:

These top three stocks currently comprise 68.7% of the ETF, which is extremely high. There are three other names that are above 5% each, and the top seven names represent 93.1% of the ETF, which is fully invested (actually, slightly over-invested).

If MSOS sees more redemptions, it will be forced to sell these three largest names, all of which dropped this week. As a reminder, in late 2022 and into 2023, MSOS saw very large redemptions. From 12/16/22 to 03/31/23, shares outstanding dropped 13.2%. At that time, MSOS owned some non-MSOs, and it used this period to eliminate those holdings.

Looking at these three large holdings, they all trade at seemingly low valuations based on the consensus at Koyfin. Curaleaf currently has an enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA in 2025 of just 4.0X. GTI trades at 4.1X, and Trulieve trades at just 2.3X. From this table below of the Top 5 MSOs, which include also Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF) and Verano (OTCQX:VRNOF), they also have low valuations for the trailing year as well as 2026:

While GTI does have positive tangible book value, the rest are negative. They all have net debt, especially Curaleaf. These companies are at risk of 280E remaining in place. Of course, EBITDA does not take their very high levels of taxation. Each of these companies is projected to have negative EPS in 2025 (CURLF: -$0.25 and TCNNF: -$0.20). GTBIF currently has a projection of $0.28 in 2025 and then $0.31 for 2026. The stock, at $5.60, has a 2026 PE of 18X.

While I upgraded GTBIF at a much higher price almost a year ago, I do not think it should be held and I am downgrading it to sell. The stock is still above its 2020 low, which is unlike any of its peers. I am not sure if it will get below $4.00, but my target for year-end is much lower at $3.72 assuming a PE of 12X for 2026. I respect the balance sheet, but the growth has been quite slow. The state-regulated cannabis system is very complex, and M&A can be quite challenging. So, the weakness across the industry won't lead to GTBIF buying out weak competitors generally unless the rules change.

I upgraded CURLF to Neutral in December, and I am downgrading it to Strong Sell. The debt level is extremely high, and the tangible book value is extremely negative at -$853 million. My target in that last article for year-end was $4.45, but I am lowering it dramatically now. In March, ahead of their Q4 financial report, I shared a target with 420 Investor subscribers of $1.35 based on 4X enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for 2026. That estimate fell sharply, and, using that same multiple, I now get $1.17, which is up 19%. This is not a lot of upside, and there is tremendous risk for this company.

I never upgraded TCNNF to Neutral, but I am lowering my rating to Strong Sell. In March, I reiterated it as a Sell when it was $4.24. In that March article, I shared a target for year-end of $7.34 based on a 4X enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA ratio. I did point out that my target may have been too aggressive due to its tax liabilities, and I am lowering that now to 3X. This works out to $4.97, which is 28% higher. I am extremely negative about Florida, which is where it does a lot of its business.

High debt levels and uncertain tax liabilities stand out for Curaleaf and Trulieve, and all three of these MSOs have big exposure to Florida. I think that their entry into beverages with THC derived from hemp could be a good thing, and am glad to see them expand beyond the dispensary-only model, but I don't think that the beverages will move the needle. Plus, the state governments are moving to shut down these types of beverages. The biggest risk to my negativity is that the federal government takes action to eliminate 280E taxation.

Yes, these stocks are all down a lot since April 30th, when the DEA announced that it was working on rescheduling:

Going back to late August 2023, their declines have been less, though all are down except for Trulieve:

Trulieve was very cheap then, which was right ahead of the rescheduling recommendation by the Department of Health & Human Services. Sure, buying dips can work, but I don't think this is yet the time to own MSOS or these three MSOs.

Conclusion

I have not gone to a Buy rating on MSOS or its three top holdings despite the big plunge in prices, but even at these lower prices I don't find any reason to own any of these. Yes, they are cheap, but this is not a reason to buy unless one thinks that things will change.

The bounce this past week, with MSOS up four straight days, is related probably to 4/20, which will be celebrated on Easter Sunday, or perhaps the introduction in the House of Representatives of STATES 2.0 Act, which was announced on 4/17 and has some good ideas. So, STATES 2.0 Act, which is just proposed in the House, might be good, but, for now, it is too early to just assume it will pass.

Again, for those who want to own cannabis stocks, I have shared many ideas here. Currently, I still believe that for those who want to own (or need to own) an ETF, CNBS is a much better option than MSOS. While I have a Buy rating on the Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), I am not so sure that it will go up a lot. It is down since that piece, but by a bit less than MSOS.

For those who want to own MSOs, I have some better ideas than CURLF, GTBIF and TCNNF. I also believe that there are opportunities outside of the MSO sector, such as some Canadian LPs and Ancillary cannabis companies. I believe in cannabis as medicine and a good alternative to alcohol, but I don't believe in all cannabis stocks all of the time. Right now, I am quite fearful for MSOS and its three largest holdings.
